ground_truth.title: Gardenary Planting vs. Ordinary Planting
ground_truth.author: Nicole Burke
ground_truth.main_content: What Is Gardenary Planting? Picture an empty 4ft. x 4ft. raised garden bed. What most gardeners would do is fill that raised bed with nine cabbage plants or maybe hundreds of carrots or dozens of garlic or onion bulbs, like the traditionally planted bed. That's not how I personally would fill those 16 square feet of growing space. I look at the bed and see a lot of missed planting opportunities. I'd put an obelisk trellis in the center of the bed and plant it with peas. Then I'd plant garlic, cilantro, dill, kale, Swiss chard, and parsley around the trellis. I'd add rows of carrots next to the large leafy greens and more rows of spinach alongside the carrots. Lastly, I'd plant chives, more herbs, and flowers around the very edge of the raised bed. The Gardenary Way Utilizes Intensive Planting. This is a style of planting called intensive planting. It may feel really strange to those of you who are used to a more traditional way of planting. It'll probably look like we've thrown the plant spacing rulebook out the window. In reality, we're still giving plants the space they need to grow, just nothing more. Packing in a wide variety of plants keeps the garden interesting. It's not just the same spaced-out peppers and beans every day for months. An intensively planted garden filled with in-season plants is constantly growing and changing. Key Differences Between Gardenary and Traditional Planting: Plant Spacing - Traditional methods follow seed packet rules designed for large row gardens. Gardenary considers mature plant size and gives each plant just enough room to grow but no more. Plant Size - Traditional planting uses couple large plants or numerous small plants in one bed. Gardenary plants a variety of different-sized plants in the same raised bed. Plant Diversity - Traditional methods plant one to two different crops per bed. Gardenary plants a wide variety to create an ecosystem, attract pollinators, fix nitrogen, repel pests, and suppress weeds. Crop Rotation - Traditional planting moves crops seasonally. Gardenary changes out many plants every 90 days through different growing seasons. Garden Tending - Traditional gardens require tending a couple times per season with clustered labor. Gardenary requires weekly pruning and support with labor spread throughout the season. Aesthetics - Traditional creates straight, uniform rows. Gardenary creates visually interesting spaces with different colors, textures, and heights. Production - Traditional harvests from single plant type after maturity. Gardenary harvests from fast-growing plants while larger plants mature. Tips to Plant the Gardenary Way: Plant in overlapping rows using stakes and twine for straight lines. Taper plants by height with smaller plants at the border and taller plants in the interior. Consider the mature size of each plant - small plants need about 1/16 to 1/9 square foot, medium plants need 1/4 square foot, and large plants need at least one square foot.
with: Picture an empty 4ft. x 4ft. raised garden bed. What most gardeners would do is fill that raised bed with nine cabbage plants or maybe hundreds of carrots or dozens of garlic or onion bulbs, like the traditionally planted bed.

The goal of a Gardenary planting plan is basically to leave no space between plants. Every square inch of the garden is covered in a delicious edible plant that will produce leaves, roots, or eventually, fruits for you to harvest and enjoy.
This is a style of planting in the garden called intensive planting. It may feel really strange to those of you who are used to a more traditional way of planting.

Plants perform best when working together with other plants. They don't like growing alone in perfectly spaced rows. When you interplant with a number of different plants, you're essentially creating a little ecosystem inside of your garden bed.
The Gardenary way of planting maximizes the production you can get from your kitchen garden space because you're growing as much as possible in however much space you have.
